strategy: agentic chunking via Gemini (headline + summary + original text)

pages.text:
To cause your body to adapt, it must receive a progressively intense training stimulus
(progressive overload). Your program has the following progression schemes to cause
continued adaptation. The progression scheme for each exercise is listed in your log.

Don't overthink these guidelines. They may look complicated at first, but they are fairly
straightforward once you understand the principles at work, namely striving for continued
progressive overload.

Linearly Progressive
This is linear progression in weight on your first work set. During your first work set, you
stick to the optimum number of reps that was determined during your test sessions (the
rep target). Every session you hit your rep target, you add the exercise's increment to
your next session's first work set weight.

Note that only your first work set of the exercise is used as benchmark of progress. How
many reps you perform in any subsequent sets does not influence whether or not you
add more weight to the exercise next session.

Example: Your bench press has 3 sets with AVT, a rep target of 11 and an increment of
2.5 kg. Last session you did 120x11, 8, 7. Next session you do 122.5x11, 7, 6. The
session after you do 125x11, 9, 6 etc.

Reactive deloading
If you could not hit your rep target during your first work set, replace any remaining sets
with low rep, explosive technique work: 3-5 reps per set at 60-70% of 1RM (roughly
equal to a weight you could do 12-20 reps with). This is speed work. If your movement
speed slows down at all during any set, you are going far too heavy.

You don't have to log the speed work, but be sure to mark the whole entry of this
exercise's session red in your spreadsheet (select the cell, select Font Color and select
a red color).

How to change a cell's font color in Excel.

In your next training session of the affected exercise, go back to the weight you
previously managed for the planned number of reps (i.e. your rep target).

Example: Your squats have 4 sets with AVT, a rep target of 7 and an increment of 5 lb.
Last session you did 345x7,5,4,3. This session you go for 350x7, but you only manage 6
reps during the first set. So for the remaining 3 sets, you do speed work. You
conservatively estimate that you could do at least 12 reps with 250, so you do 250x3,3,3
as a reactive deload. You note down 350x6 in your log and next session you go back to
345x7 before retrying 350x7.

If you're interested in the rationale behind reactive deloading compared to more
traditional deloading methods, read this article.

Autoregulated progression
If, after performing the planned number of reps with the planned weight during set 1, you
think you'll be able to perform that number of reps again with a higher weight in
subsequent sets, go for it. As long as you think you can keep hitting your rep target,
keep increasing the weight to progress as fast as you can.

Example: Your Romanian deadlifts have a rep target of 6, 4 sets with AVT and an
increment of 1 kg. Last session you did 180x6,5,4,3, so this session you are scheduled
to do 181x6. Set 1 turns out to be easy. You estimate that you could do 182.5x6, so you
do. You still didn't come anywhere near failure, so in your third set you go for 190x6.
That took everything you got, so in set 4, you stick with 190 and you happen to get 3
reps. You note down 181x6, 182.5x6, 190x6,3

Summary
As long as you keep hitting your rep target for an exercise in its first work set, Linearly
Progressive comes down to linearly increasing the weight every session by the
increment. If you don't hit your rep target in your first work set, implement a reactive
deload. If you can make faster progress than 1 increment per session, go for it.

Rep Range Progression
Rep Range Progression is similar to Linearly Progressive in that your first set is your
progression benchmark and your goal over time is to perform your rep target with more
and more weight. However, during Rep Range Progression, you're not expected to hit
your rep target every workout. Instead, you'll generally experience a decrease in reps
when you increase the weight and then you'll progress in reps back up to your rep
target, when you increase the weight again.

Thus, you normally progress either in weight or in reps. If you hit your rep target in your
first work set, you increase the weight by the increment next session. If you don't hit your
rep target yet, you stick with the same weight next session and try to perform more reps
with it next session.

Autoregulated Progression
Just like during Linearly Progressive, if you're confident can progress in weight faster
than a single increment per session, go for it.

Example for Bayesian curls with a rep target of 15, 3 sets, and an increment of 1 kg:
Session 1: 20x15, 13, 11
Session 2: 21x15, 15, 14
Session 3: 22x15, 23x15,13 (autoregulated progress as above to progress faster than
planned)
Session 4: 24x13, 10, 8
Session 5: 24x14, 10, 9
Session 6: 24x15, 13, 12
Session 7: 25x10, 9, 8
Session 8: 25x12, 9, 7
Session 9: 25x15, 11, 9
Session 10: 26x10, 8, 7

etc.

Plateau breakers
Whenever you don't progress as planned in a workout, in the next workout increase the
weight by ~10%, up to a maximum weight of ~3RM for compound exercises and ~5RM
for isolation exercises. This session is a plateau breaker. The lower volume, high
intensity workout allows for dissipation of fatigue and an increase in strength. After that,
go back down an increment and try to increase your reps again. Repeat if needed.

Example for Bayesian curls with a rep target of 15, 1 set, and an increment of 1 kg:
15x15
16x10
16x10
17x7
16x11
16x15
17x9
17x9
18x6
17x10
etc.

Reactive deloads
When you are clearly underperforming and this is likely due to not having recovered fully
from your last workout, you should implement a reactive deload, in addition to the usual
plateau breaker.

Concretely, if you perform fewer rather than more reps than last time you performed an
exercise in the first set, perform a reactive deload. The deload functions exactly like for

Linearly Progressive progression. For example, if you did 85x7 last time, you should
achieve 85x8 to progress. If you can only manage 6 or fewer reps, perform a reactive
deload.

When you increase the weight, you will not have a planned number of reps to perform. If
your reps end up at 4 or below in the first set, go back to the weight you used last
workout for set 2 (if you have a second set). You can then use the second set to see if
you should implement a reactive deload: if you can manage 2+ fewer reps than last
workout, do the reactive deload.
For example, if you did 85x11,9 last time and in today's workout you do 87.5x4, go back
to 85 in set 2. If you can only manage 7 or fewer reps instead of the 9 you did last time,
perform a reactive deload

Summary
Rep Range Progression comes down to trying to hit your rep target with continuously
more weight in your first work set. In sessions after you hit your rep target, you increase
the weight by the increment (or you already increase the weight within the same session
if you think you can still hit your rep target then). In sessions after you didn't hit your rep
target, you work up in reps until you do. If you plateau, implement a plateau breaking
session before trying to increase your reps again. If your performance is 2+ reps poorer
than planned, perform a reactive deload that session in addition to the plateau breaker
next session.

Undulating Linearly Progressive
This is equal to 2 alternating, independent Linearly Progressive schemes. So you
alternate training sessions with a lower and a higher rep target progression track. These
2 tracks function exactly like 2 independent Linearly Progressive schemes.

Example sessions progression for the overhead press with a 2.5 kg increment, one set
and rep targets of 8 and 3 with color coding to illustrate the alternating rep targets:
60x8, 70x3, 62.5x8, 72.5x3, 65x8, 75x3

Undulating Rep Range Progression
Analogous to Undulating Periodization in relation to Linearly Progressive, Undulating
Rep Range Progression is equal to 2 alternating, independent rep range progression
schemes. So you alternate between sessions with a lower and a higher rep target for the
exercise in question.

Example sessions progression for chin-ups with a 5 lb increment, one set and
Undulating Rep Range progression with rep targets of 8 and 12 with color coding to
illustrate the alternating rep targets:
10x8, 20x3, 10x12, 20x4, 15x8, 20x6, 15x12, 20x8, 20x9, 25x6

Set-rep Range Progression
This is progression in reps across sets. Set-rep Range Progression functions like Rep
Range progression except that you now have to reach the rep target in all sets instead
of only in your first set and you're adhering to a fixed rest interval by using a
timer/stopwatch/phone.

Example Set-rep Range Progression during squats with a 10 lb increment, a rep target
of 3 and 4 sets:
300x3,3,3,3
310x3,3,3,3
320x3,3,3,3
330x3,3,3,3
etc.

Reactive deloading
If you fail to hit the rep target during any set, you stop the exercise and continue with the
same weight next session until you've completed the rep target during every set. If you
do not progress in reps next session after the reactive deload, perform a 10% weight
reset: decrease the weight by 10% the session after the plateau and proceed as
planned from there on again.

Be strict with your rest intervals: you have to time them with a stopwatch or your phone.
If you cheat by resting longer and longer than planned, you're faking your own progress.

Example Set-rep Range Progression during squats with a 10 lb increment, a rep target
of 3 and 4 sets:
300x3,3,3,3
310x3,3,2
310x3,3,3,3

320x3,1
320x3,3,2
320x3,3,3,3
330x3,3,3,1
330x3,3,3,3
etc.

Autoregulated progression
If you're very confident that you can reach the rep target during every set with a higher
weight than planned, go for it.

Example Set-rep Range Progression during deadlifts with a 10 lb increment, a rep target
of 3 and 4 sets:
300x3,3,3,3
310x3 (easy), 320x3 (still easy), 330x3,3
340x3,3,3,3
etc.

Summary
Set-rep Range Progression comes down to trying to hit your rep target in all your sets
while employing a strictly timed rest interval. When you've hit your rep target in all sets
with a certain weight, you increase the weight by the increment. If you don't hit your rep
target in any set, skip your remaining sets and try to hit your rep target in more sets next
session. If you plateau, reduce the weight by 10% and work back up.

Clustered Linearly Progressive
This is Linearly Progressive periodization with an intra-set rest interval (cluster sets): you
rest not just in between sets but also in between clusters. A cluster is a series of reps.
The notation and progression rules are the same as for Linearly Progressive.

For example, x3x5 Clustered with a 30 second inter-cluster rest interval means you
perform sets of 15 reps with 30 seconds of rust in between every 3 reps.
So with 80 kg each set looks like this.
80x3
30 seconds rest
80x3
30 seconds rest
etc. until you reach 15 reps.

PAP Progression
Post-activation potentiation (PAP) is a phenomenon where heavy strength training (1-
5RM usually) induces more performance-enhancing changes in the nervous system and
the exercised muscle's architecture than it induces fatigue. As a result, after certain
(near-)maximal muscle contractions at specific times, you actually become stronger in
spite of being fatigued. We can take advantage of this by performing a heavy single in
between lighter sets.

Concretely, set 1 is a 1RM. This is your benchmark. If your 1RM didn't increase, perform
sets of only 3 reps with 80% for your remaining sets and go back 1 increment in weight
next session (reactive deload similar to Linearly Progressive). If your 1RM did increase
as planned, proceed with the following sets as follows.
Set 2 is performed with 80% of that 1RM, 1 rep to failure.
Set 3 is a single with 90% of 1RM.
Set 4 is performed with 80% of 1RM again.
Repeat alternating between singles with 90 and sets 80% for any remaining sets left in
your program.

Example:
Set 1: 100 kg x 1 rep
Set 2: 80 kg x 8 reps
Set 3: 90 kg x 1 rep
Set 4: 80 kg x 7 reps

Myo Rep Range
Myo-reps are a rest-pause training method consisting of a 'primer set' and subsequent
'volume sets'. The primer set is a straight set with a rep count equal to your rep target,
performed with 2 full repetitions in reserve. Directly after the primer set, take 5 deep
breaths and perform another set with only a single repetition in reserve. Repeat the
volume sets until you reach the set volume indicated in your log. During the last set,
leave no repetitions in reserve: perform every grinder you possibly can.

Example myo-rep set with a rep target of 20: 50 kg x 20 reps, 6 reps, 5 reps, 4 reps.

Your primer set is your benchmark set in terms of how to progress, exactly as for Rep
Range progression. After you've hit your rep target with any weight in your primer set,
increase your working weight by the increment next session. If you don't hit your rep
target the first time again with the higher weight, move up in reps until you do. If you get
stuck on a certain weight x rep combination, increase the weight next session (plateau
breaker) and then go back to the plateau to break it the session after.
